title: Carbon and nitrogen nutrient analysis of eucalyptus leaves
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"guugj","text":"This protocol aims to record total carbon and nitrogen as a percentage of total leaf mass using combustion analysis. The protocol uses ground leaf material and has been tested on a variety of shrub and tree species. The protocol is straightforward and easy to complete once trained in the use of the applicable combustion analyser. Depending on the type of columns in the combustion analyser, carbon can consume some components readily increasing the frequency of column replacement which can increase the time taken and cost to complete this method. The key steps of this method including grinding the dried plant material down to consistent size, for example 1 mm, and then analysing the samples using a combustion analyser. Grinding can take up to three hours for 40 samples and weighing samples for, and running, the analyser can take 7-8 hours.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dpegz","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"tfceg","text":"Carbon is crucial to understand when assessing plant traits (Ma et al. 2018). Nitrogen is also important as it can be a limiting factor for ecosystem productivity (LeBauer and Treseder 2008). Nitrogen is also an important consideration in forest fuels as it can reduce flammability of plants by altering the pyrolysis pathway (Grootemaat et al. 2015). Whilst this protocol is easy to complete, the type of machine will affect the costs or running each sample. The temperature at which samples are dried is an important consideration. We assessed carbon and nitrogen content of both fresh leaves and dried leaves. Standard procedure recommends a drying temperature of 60°C to remove moisture over a number of days but to prevent the decomposition of any nutrients (Pérez-Harguindeguy et al. 2013). We tested the effects of drying at 105°C and 60°C on eucalyptus leaves and found that there were no significant differences in nitrogen values but that final carbon content values differed between the two drying temperatures. However, as we were required to dry samples at 105°C for moisture content measurements (Matthews 2010), we proceeded with grinding the samples dried at higher temperatures for C and N analyses for efficiency. Values recorded for samples dried at 105°C are sufficient to detect differences between species and individuals. See the protocol “Estimation of Fuel Moisture Content (FMC), Equivalent water thickness (EWT), Dry Matter Content (DMC), and Specific Leaf Area (SLA) in eucalypt leaves” for further instructions on how to collect and dry leaves (Younes and Cain 2023).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bnhpn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: For Cyclotec: grind dried leaves to 1 mm diameter in a fume hood using a laboratory mill by placing leaves into entry funnel and push down using a dowel shaped object. For cryogenic grinder: tear one dried leaf into pieces and place in vial along with four metal grinding beads and grind in two pulses of 30 seconds each. NOTE: Be careful to adhere to any safety warnings around the grinder and ensure you are properly trained in the use of the equipment. Do not place hands into the funnel, near the grinding blade or into the cryogenic chamber except for when placing samples.
Place ground plant material into sample jars for storage.
Clean cyclotec grinder between samples using pressurised air and ethanol.

Place 0.05-0.2 g of ground plant material into crucible. Lower amounts of plant material will increase the life of the consumable column components. Test the lower detection limit of the analyser for your species of interest by measuring C and N at various weights of plant material.
Analyse for total carbon and total nitrogen using a carbon and nitrogen analyser according to the manufacturer’s instructions.
Figure 1 includes some troubleshooting advice to assist with this protocol.

Grinding oven dried leaves – 40 samples processed in 3 hours. Analysing plant material for C and N – 7-8 hours for 60 samples. <ul> <li>Weight out leaf material into crucibles, 1.5 hours for 60 samples.</li> <li>Run analyser, time varies depending on analyser but approximately 6 hours for 60 samples in the authors experience.</li> </ul>

The reported average for leaf carbon content globally is 46.9% (Ma et al. 2018). A range of values around this average could be expected for healthy plants, for plants undergoing drought values should be considerably lower.
Figure 1. Figure 1: Troublshooting advice for analysis of carbon and nitrogen in Eucalyptus leaves.
